known_projects.project: agentic fullstack operating system · local-first AI agents
AI speaking practice · solo reps with scored feedback
information-diet interventions for short-form video · TEE-delegated watch history
Shake is a social network app that connects people when they shake their phones together.

known_technologies.tech: DStack's workload identifier (alternative to compose hash)
Linux distribution base for several cohort projects
C++ runtime for running LLM models locally
Confidential compute network providing CVM execution

example.chunk: [Hang Yin] when I was working on the service mesh, there is decision I need to make. So, how should we define the policy? One way is that we verify the exact code running in each CVM, so eventually it become we define a few compose hash, and we have a white list of the compose hash, then if the compose hashes match, we allow them to join this service mesh. And another way is we can verify the app ID, because the app ID is the unit you define the workload in DStack. But app ID, I didn't go with app ID, because that will make the deployment a little bit harder. I have to define the app ID first, then define the policy and wire everything together. With compose hash, I can pre calculate all the measurements before the deployment, and then just deploy it once.
example.expected: { "summary": "Hang Yin chose compose-hash-based policy over app-ID-based policy for the DStack service mesh; compose-hash lets measurements be pre-calculated and the deployment ships in one step instead of needing to wire app ID, policy, and code together.", "signals": [ {"kind": "action_item", "text": "Use compose-hash whitelisting (not app-ID whitelisting) for the DStack service mesh policy", "source_quote": "I didn't go with app ID, because that will make the deployment a little bit harder", "said_by": ["Hang Yin"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Compose-hash whitelisting lets all measurements be pre-calculated before deployment, so the policy ships in a single deploy step", "source_quote": "I can pre calculate all the measurements before the deployment, and then just deploy it once", "said_by": ["Hang Yin"], "about_person": []} ], "entities": [ {"name": "DStack", "type": "project", "evidence": "the service mesh he's defining policy for"}, {"name": "service mesh", "type": "concept", "evidence": "the system whose policy is being defined"}, {"name": "compose hash", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the chosen whitelisting key"}, {"name": "app ID", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the rejected alternative whitelisting key"}, {"name": "CVM", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the unit each compose hash measures"}, {"name": "KMS", "type": "technology", "evidence": "guarantee app ID would have provided automatically"} ], "topics": ["attestation", "service mesh", "deployment"] }
example.lessons: - v2.2: When a speaker walks through a decision they personally made, that's an "action_item" signal (which absorbed the old "decision" kind). The trigger phrases ("there is decision I need to make", "I didn't go with X because Y") are explicit action_item markers — DO NOT default them to "insight." - Multiple distinct entity types in one chunk: project (DStack, service mesh), technology (compose hash, app ID, CVM, KMS). Don't lump everything into "concept" — extract the specific type each time. - Two related signals (the action_item AND the reasoning) can come from one chunk. The reasoning is its own "insight" — don't merge them into one diluted signal.

example.chunk: [Alex (flashbots?)] I will send you the link to the specific project. It's github.com/easy te [Shaw] easy te easy hyphen te, [Alex (flashbots?)] yeah, if you're forking tails, this will make your life a lot easier, because I consider forking tails, and I went down that rabbit hole for like three months
example.expected: { "summary": "Alex (Flashbots) offered to share the EZTE GitHub link with Shaw, after Shaw mentioned he is forking Tails for his agent OS work.", "signals": [ {"kind": "action_item", "text": "Alex will send Shaw the github.com/easy-te link", "source_quote": "I will send you the link to the specific project. It's github.com/easy te", "said_by": ["Alex (flashbots?)"], "about_person": ["Shaw"]}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Tails forking is a 3-month rabbit hole; EZTE compresses that to a simpler workflow", "source_quote": "I went down that rabbit hole for like three months", "said_by": ["Alex (flashbots?)"], "about_person": []} ], "entities": [ {"name": "EZTE", "type": "project", "evidence": "github.com/easy-te shared as the project link"}, {"name": "Tails", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the Debian-based live OS Shaw is forking"}, {"name": "Alex (flashbots?)", "type": "person", "evidence": "speaker offering the link", "affiliation": "flashbots"}, {"name": "Shaw", "type": "person", "evidence": "recipient of the link, forking Tails"} ], "topics": ["reproducible builds", "agent operating systems"] }
example.lessons: - said_by ("Alex (flashbots?)") and about_person ("Shaw") are DIFFERENT — Alex offers, Shaw receives. Always fill about_person when there's a clear addressee, not just when there's a third party mentioned. - External speakers (Alex's "(flashbots?)" parenthetical) keep their LABEL verbatim in said_by but the AFFILIATION field on the corresponding Person entity captures the hint. The base name "Alex" stays as the entity name; "flashbots" goes in affiliation. - Each Person who's a speaker AND a named entity in the chunk should appear in entities[], not just resolved_speakers. Multiple person entities is fine.

example.chunk: [Andrew Miller] Does anyone here done anything interesting with cross-chain bridges? How about sending any transactions from a tee? Oh, yeah, Demers has a hy Tina [Andrew Miller] Tina. Also, I think is in New York already, just quite here in person.
example.expected: { "summary": "Andrew Miller asked the room for experience with cross-chain bridges and TEE-originated transactions; question left open within this chunk. He also noted Tina is in New York but not yet physically present.", "signals": [ {"kind": "open_question", "text": "Has anyone done interesting work with cross-chain bridges or sending transactions from a TEE?", "source_quote": "Does anyone here done anything interesting with cross-chain bridges? How about sending any transactions from a tee?", "said_by": ["Andrew Miller"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Tina is in New York already but not yet physically present at the meeting", "source_quote": "Tina. Also, I think is in New York already, just quite here in person", "said_by": ["Andrew Miller"], "about_person": ["Tina"]} ], "entities": [ {"name": "cross-chain bridges", "type": "technology", "evidence": "subject of the open question"}, {"name": "TEE", "type": "technology", "evidence": "subject of the open question"}, {"name": "Andrew Miller", "type": "person", "evidence": "asked the open question"}, {"name": "Tina", "type": "person", "evidence": "mentioned as in New York but not present at the meeting"} ], "topics": ["cross-chain", "confidential compute"] }
example.lessons: - A question is an "open_question" ONLY when it's NOT answered in the same chunk. If the speaker self-answers, it's an insight, not an open_question. - Cross-cohort references (Tina being "in New York") generate an "insight" with the mentioned person in about_person — even when Tina isn't speaking. The chunk is asserting a logistical fact about her, so she's the subject. - Multi-clause chunks can carry two signals from one speaker: the open question + a separate insight. Don't artificially limit to one signal per speaker.

example.chunk: [Albiona Hoti] The actual user journey is basically get on the app, you select the different themes of topics that you want to talk about, and the depth, and then you read for one to two minutes, and you record yourself on the app speaking about it. The app used to give you only an overview of basically the awards, closet structure, pace and time, and the delivery on organization, security, language, all of these little details, but then now added another session where we evaluate also the content of what we're speaking, because people were saying like saying a bunch of other things the content itself, and I didn't check that in the beginning, and now it also checks the content itself. For now I have 524 users, out of which our users are hundreds of things, or I think people who have done more density sessions.
example.expected: { "summary": "Albiona shipped a content-quality evaluation pass on Elocute on top of the existing delivery-quality metrics (pace, structure, organization, language); the app now scores both how you say it AND what you said. Currently has 524 users.", "signals": [ {"kind": "action_item", "text": "Added a content-evaluation pass to Elocute on top of the existing delivery-evaluation metrics", "source_quote": "now added another session where we evaluate also the content of what we're speaking", "said_by": ["Albiona Hoti"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Users were unhappy with delivery-only scoring because they could game it by talking a lot without saying much", "source_quote": "people were saying like saying a bunch of other things the content itself", "said_by": ["Albiona Hoti"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Elocute has reached 524 users, with a denser power-user segment doing repeated sessions", "source_quote": "For now I have 524 users", "said_by": ["Albiona Hoti"], "about_person": []} ], "entities": [ {"name": "Elocute", "type": "project", "evidence": "the app being presented"}, {"name": "Albiona Hoti", "type": "person", "evidence": "the presenter"}, {"name": "articulation", "type": "concept", "evidence": "the core skill the app trains"} ], "topics": ["product evolution", "articulation", "speaking practice"] }
example.lessons: - Even in a long pitch monologue, a single chunk can yield multiple distinct signals (a decision + the insight motivating it + a metric milestone). Don't collapse them into one bland summary. - Concrete numbers ("524 users") become "insight" signals — they're not decisions, not actions, but they're the kind of fact someone preparing for a follow-up meeting needs. - Summary should LEAD with what was shipped/decided, not "Albiona talked about Elocute." The DO/DON'T contrast in the prompt applies to summaries inside chunks too.

example.chunk: [Andrew Miller] The last problem that this solves is just like I now appreciate the importance of human water coolers, or people who act as health connections. My exemplar for this is Tita, who you'll get to meet later tonight or tomorrow. She's been my entrepreneurship mentor since I dropped out of university to work on TV-based with Chevy, and the mode at which she works — she's like your most effective human router that anyone who knows her has seen. This is someone who makes it their job to pick up context about everyone that they can in their orbit, often through just like very effective prompting.
example.expected: { "summary": "Andrew framed the Router project around the 'human water cooler' role — people who absorb and route context across a community — using Tita, his entrepreneurship mentor, as the canonical exemplar.", "signals": [ {"kind": "insight", "text": "The 'human water cooler' archetype — someone who absorbs and effectively routes context across a community — is what the Router project is trying to instrument with software", "source_quote": "I now appreciate the importance of human water coolers, or people who act as health connections", "said_by": ["Andrew Miller"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "Tita is the canonical example of a high-effectiveness human router; she will be at the cohort tonight or tomorrow", "source_quote": "My exemplar for this is Tita, who you'll get to meet later tonight or tomorrow", "said_by": ["Andrew Miller"], "about_person": ["Tita"]} ], "entities": [ {"name": "Router", "type": "project", "evidence": "the project being motivated"}, {"name": "Tita", "type": "person", "evidence": "named exemplar of a human-router role; Andrew's entrepreneurship mentor"}, {"name": "Andrew Miller", "type": "person", "evidence": "the speaker/presenter"}, {"name": "human water cooler", "type": "concept", "evidence": "Andrew's framing for the archetype the Router project targets"} ], "topics": ["context routing", "community building"] }
example.lessons: - A speaker can talk extensively ABOUT someone NOT in the meeting (Tita). Extract that person as a "person" entity AND put them in about_person on the relevant signal. Tita not being a speaker doesn't disqualify her. - Coined concepts ("human water cooler") are entities of type "concept" — the speaker's framing IS extractable, not just named tools and projects. - "My exemplar for X is Y" patterns yield an insight about Y, not just an insight about X. Specificity wins.

example.chunk: [Andrew Miller] Do you have on the demo that you said, do you have, like, an up and running service version of this? Like, is this something that runs on, like, an Ethereum testnet or something? [Chloe Wang] No, it's currently not deployed, it's only running local.
example.lessons: - Speaker N labels are NEVER promoted to real names — keep them verbatim in said_by even when the surrounding context might let you guess. - Q-and-A patterns where the answer is in the same chunk are NOT open_questions — they're insights carrying the revealed information. The signal text describes the answer, not the question. - A negative answer ("not deployed yet, only running local") is still high-signal — it's the prep-relevant fact a future organizer needs.

example.chunk: [Andrew Hang] Thankfully, Opus 4.0 came out and solved a bunch of our headaches, so we were working on this for a year before we really got into traction, and then, yeah, January this year finally teams started using Cloud Code and MCP is a lot more, so we've gotten a lot of usage since then. Now we've moved into the harness layer. Over the last year and a half of working on this, we've migrated frameworks like seven or eight times, whether that's from Lang Chaining to Land Graph to BAM, to Mastra to AI SDK, now to Asia to SDK — it's like a painful migration every time, so we've now learned to like try and not over-engineer and overfit the work.
example.expected: { "summary": "Andrew Hang's team finally got traction after Opus 4.0 + Cloud Code + MCP adoption in January; they've migrated agent-framework stacks 7-8 times (LangChain → LangGraph → BAML → Mastra → AI SDK → ASI SDK) and have learned to avoid over-engineering.", "signals": [ {"kind": "insight", "text": "Andrew Hang's MCP block explorer got production traction once Opus 4.0, Cloud Code, and MCP adoption converged in January — about a year of work before that", "source_quote": "January this year finally teams started using Cloud Code and MCP is a lot more, so we've gotten a lot of usage since then", "said_by": ["Andrew Hang"], "about_person": []}, {"kind": "insight", "text": "After 7-8 framework migrations across the agent stack, Andrew Hang's team has learned to avoid over-engineering and over-fitting solutions to current frameworks", "source_quote": "we've migrated frameworks like seven or eight times … so we've now learned to like try and not over-engineer", "said_by": ["Andrew Hang"], "about_person": []} ], "entities": [ {"name": "Opus 4.x", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the model release that unblocked their traction"}, {"name": "Cloud Code", "type": "technology", "evidence": "the agent runtime teams started adopting"}, {"name": "MCP", "type": "protocol", "evidence": "their protocol surface that got adoption"}, {"name": "LangChain", "type": "technology", "evidence": "first framework in the migration list"}, {"name": "LangGraph", "type": "technology", "evidence": "second framework in the migration list"}, {"name": "BAML", "type": "technology", "evidence": "third framework in the migration list"}, {"name": "Mastra", "type": "technology", "evidence": "fourth framework in the migration list"}, {"name": "Vercel AI SDK", "type": "technology", "evidence": "fifth framework in the migration list"}, {"name": "ASI SDK", "type": "technology", "evidence": "current framework choice"}, {"name": "Andrew Hang", "type": "person", "evidence": "the speaker walking through the framework history"} ], "topics": ["agent runtime", "framework selection", "production traction"] }
example.lessons: - THE OPEN-WORLD RULE IN ACTION: BAML, Mastra, Vercel AI SDK, ASI SDK, Cloud Code are NOT in the team_context anchor lists — but they're named in the transcript and they're real, so extract them. The anchor list helps you name LangChain/LangGraph/MCP/Opus 4.x canonically; it does NOT limit which entities you extract. - A chunk densely-packed with technology names should yield a high-count technology entity list. Don't collapse "we migrated through 5 frameworks" into a single "frameworks (concept)" entity — name each one. - When a speaker enumerates a list of related items, each item is its own entity. Lists are recall opportunities, not items to abstract away.

example.chunk: [Alex (flashbots?)] yeah, I can give you my email if you, if you run into any issues [Shaw] By the way, if you want to send me an email, the best email to reach me is Alex at Alex holbrook dot com
example.expected: { "summary": "Alex (Flashbots) offered to share his email with Shaw IF Shaw runs into issues with EZTE; Shaw reciprocated by sharing his own email (Alex at Alex holbrook dot com).", "signals": [ {"kind": "action_item", "text": "Alex (Flashbots) will give Shaw his email IF Shaw runs into issues using EZTE", "source_quote": "I can give you my email if you, if you run into any issues", "said_by": ["Alex (flashbots?)"], "about_person": ["Shaw"]}, {"kind": "action_item", "text": "Shaw shared his contact email (Alex at Alex holbrook dot com) — an open invitation, not conditional", "source_quote": "the best email to reach me is Alex at Alex holbrook dot com", "said_by": ["Shaw"], "about_person": ["Alex (flashbots?)"]} ], "entities": [ {"name": "EZTE", "type": "project", "evidence": "the project whose use is the implicit trigger for Alex's email offer"}, {"name": "Alex (flashbots?)", "type": "person", "evidence": "speaker offering the conditional contact", "affiliation": "flashbots"}, {"name": "Shaw", "type": "person", "evidence": "speaker offering reciprocal unconditional contact"} ], "topics": ["collaboration", "follow-up coordination"] }
example.lessons: - THE CONDITIONALITY RULE IN ACTION: "I can give you my email IF you run into issues" is NOT the same signal as "I'll give you my email." Keep the IF clause in the text — it's what makes the signal useful for prep ("Shaw, did you actually run into issues? You can email Alex if so"). - Side-by-side conditional and unconditional action items in one chunk: extract BOTH, mark the difference. The model often defaults to flattening — don't. - said_by and about_person SWAP across the two signals: in the first, Alex acts and Shaw is the addressee. In the second, Shaw acts and Alex is the addressee. Both should appear.

example.chunk: [Tina Z] some of the insights that maybe I think are welcome for this particular cohort — I think that the party experience I'm more familiar with is when you were at Azuki, which is quite interesting as your consumer went through this column theory. What were some of the lessons of case study, and also if any additional progress had any pipeline for folks who are more in identifying what is product market fit for them in like this multi-agent centric world?
example.lessons: - Azuki is NOT in the team_context anchor list — but it's a real NFT project Shaw worked at, named in the transcript, so extract it as a "project". This is the open-world rule on external entities. - When a question is directed AT a specific person ("what lessons did YOU take from Azuki"), about_person captures that addressee. said_by is the asker, about_person is the addressee. Both are filled when there's a clear direction. - Topics can come from the QUESTION-asking context, not just from named entities. "Consumer go-to-market" is a topic even though it's not literally a project or technology.

style_guide.kind: Anyone commits to a course of action. Group OR individual, soft OR hard. Triggers include "I'll send", "you handle", "we should X", "let's go with Y", "I'm going to try Z", "let me know when W". Conditionals MUST be preserved verbatim ("Alex will help IF asked" ≠ "Alex will help"). Person-attached when possible. ABSORBED the old "decision" kind in v2.2.

A question raised in this chunk that is NOT answered within the same chunk. Includes implicit ones ("the thing we still need to figure out is X"). Excludes rhetorical questions ("you know?", "right?") and fully-answered ones.

A notable nugget: specific, praiseworthy, or a synthesised observation across a stretch of dialogue. Three soft tests: (1) self-contained, (2) names at least one entity, (3) synthesis over single-sentence paraphrase. A sharp single-sentence praise IS an insight if it's notable and named. CAP: max 4 per chunk. ABSORBED the old "insight" kind in v2.2.

team_context.open_world_note: The lists above are NON-EXHAUSTIVE. New projects, technologies, people (including guests joining a single meeting), and topics WILL appear in transcripts and must be extracted faithfully even when not listed here. Treat the lists as ANCHORS for known entities, not as a closed vocabulary.
